next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

At her desk in Keating, Leela was trying to coax one dead benchmark run into becoming a paragraph she could still someday call a dissertation when the email from Research Integrity arrived. Outside the sixth-floor window, Lakebridge had gone the color of dishwater, the wet part of cold rather than snow, a steady damp that climbed into coat sleeves and stayed there. The message carried a cheerful blue flag beside it, as if someone had taken the time to mark it important by hand.

Subject: Required Attendance - Research Integrity Intake

Dear Ms. Krishnan,

Please report to Baines Hall, Room 414, at 2:30 p.m. today regarding a data-handling concern associated with privileged access credentials under your stewardship.

The message was signed by Mara Sloane, Associate Director, and copied an Information Security address that looked automated enough to absolve everybody in advance.

Leela read it twice, then a third time with her hand flat on the desk so the tendons in her wrist would stop twitching under the brace. The phrase privileged access credentials under your stewardship sat in her head like a stain. Nobody in Keating said things like stewardship unless they were already arranging blame into folders.

Across the cubicle wall Omar said, "You made the face."

He had a pharmacy bag open on his desk and was eating pistachios one by one, setting the empty shells in a neat line along the edge of a conference flyer. Even hungry, even frightened, he organized his mess before it existed. He leaned back enough to see her over the partition, his beard line still too ca...
open_hooks_to_carry: Shift the novel's rhythm from fluorescent public shame to greasy late-night aftermath.
Use humor and appetite as forms of temporary reprieve, not as tone-breaking gags.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e ugly yellow cells where the held-out numbers sank. Nobody interrupted now. They watched her narrow her own claim in public. Professor Adler wrote without looking up. The policy professor had turned his plate upside down on his folder so the grease wouldn't get on the paper. Omar sat bent over his printout, staring at it hard enough to suggest the sheet itself might clear him.

"When the split is clean," June said, touching the laser dot to the table, "the improvement is modest and local. Fine. Then the honest claim is local. The benchmark still tells you something useful. It just doesn't get to swagger."

A chair leg screeched at the back. One of the first-years slipped out with half a cardamom knot wrapped in a napkin, trying for stealth and failing. Ben had started typing again, no longer pretending not to.

June clicked back to the architecture slide. "Model stack is the same. Thread context. Author-link heuristics. Error slices by euphemism family. None of that vanishes because the cleaner split takes a bat to the pretty number."

Halfway through the next sentence her voice caught. She cleared it and kept going. On the second catch, the postdoc with the baby-sticker bottle stood, crossed the aisle, and set the bottle beside June's paper cup without a word.

The whole room saw it.

June stopped for exactly one beat, nodded once without looking up, unscrewed the stranger's bottle with her free hand, and drank. Then she braced her left hand on the lectern and kept explaining the architecture while Professor Adler wrote down the smaller claim as June's headline all along.
state_deltas_required: Leela and Omar try to interpret the seminar disaster and decide whether Mohit's behavior is strategic cruelty or simply ordinary academic bad faith.
They form a partial alliance built on incomplete truth, which helps them act together later but leaves a fracture inside the alliance from the start.
